title: Detecting and Correcting Sample-by-Sample Scale Distortion in RNA Sequencing Data
summary: RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) is the conventional genome-scale approach used to capture the expression levels of all detectable genes in a biological sample. This is now regularly used for population-based studies designed to identify genetic determinants of various diseases. Naturally, the accuracy of these tests should be verified and improved if possible. In this study, we aimed to detect and correct for expression level-dependent errors which vary from sample to sample, and are not corrected by conventional normalization techniques . We examined several RNA-seq datasets from the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), Stand Up 2 Cancer (SU2C), and GTEx databases with various types of preprocessing. By applying local averaging, we found sample by sample expression-level dependent biases in all datasets studied. Using simulations, we show that these biases corrupt gene-gene correlation estimations and $t$ tests between subpopulations. To mitigate these biases, we introduce two different nonlinear transforms based on statistical considerations that correct these observed biases. We demonstrate that that these transforms effectively remove the observed per-sample biases, reduce sample-to-sample variance, and improve the characteristics of gene-gene correlation distributions. Using a novel simulation methodology that creates controlled differences between subpopulations, we show that these transforms reduce variability and increase sensitivity of two population tests. The improvements in sensitivity and specificity were of the order of 3-5\% in most instances after the data was corrected for bias. Altogether, these results improve our capacity to understand gene-gene relationships, and may lead to novel ways to utilize the information derived from clinical tests.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies and corrects sample-specific, expression-level-dependent biases in RNA-seq data that persist after conventional normalization. Using local averaging across TCGA, SU2C, and GTEx datasets, the authors demonstrate these biases corrupt correlation estimates and t-tests, then introduce two nonlinear statistical transforms that reduce variance and improve sensitivity/specificity by 3-5% in population comparisons.
AI.motivation: RNA-seq is widely used in population studies to identify genetic disease determinants, but conventional normalization techniques fail to correct expression-level-dependent errors that vary sample-by-sample, potentially compromising downstream analyses like gene-gene correlation and differential expression testing.
AI.method: Local averaging was applied to detect per-sample expression-level-dependent biases across multiple RNA-seq datasets (TCGA, SU2C, GTEx). Two nonlinear statistical transforms were developed to correct these biases, and their effectiveness was validated through simulations with controlled subpopulation differences.
AI.result: Expression-level-dependent biases were found in all datasets studied. These biases corrupt gene-gene correlation estimates and t-tests between subpopulations. The proposed nonlinear transforms effectively remove per-sample biases, reduce sample-to-sample variance, and improve correlation distributions. Sensitivity and specificity improved by approximately 3-5% after correction.
AI.conclusion: Correcting expression-level-dependent biases improves the accuracy of population-level RNA-seq analyses, enhances understanding of gene-gene relationships, and could lead to better utilization of clinical RNA-seq data for disease research.

title: ROI Extraction in Thermographic Breast Images Using Genetic Algorithms
summary: This work proposes the use of Genetic Algorithms (GA) to identify the area of the breast from the background in thermographic breast images. The proposed method uses color information, a fitness function based on cardioids, and GA. This is the first work in the literature to propose a Region of Interest (ROI) extraction based on GA and cariods. ROI extraction can improve the accuracy of cancer detection and assist with the standardization of acquisition protocols. The method is able to successfully separate the breast region in 52 out of 58 images, while being fully automatic, and not requiring manual selection of seed points.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Genetic Algorithm-based method using cardioid-shaped fitness functions to automatically extract breast regions from thermographic images, achieving successful segmentation in 52 out of 58 images without manual seed point selection.
AI.motivation: To improve breast cancer detection accuracy and standardize acquisition protocols by automatically extracting the Region of Interest (breast area) from thermographic images, eliminating the need for manual seed point selection.
AI.method: The method employs Genetic Algorithms with color information and a cardioid-based fitness function to automatically identify and separate the breast region from the background in thermographic breast images.
AI.result: The method successfully separated the breast region in 52 out of 58 images (approximately 90% accuracy), operating fully automatically without requiring manual seed point selection.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work to propose ROI extraction using GA and cardioids for thermographic breast images, offering a fully automatic approach that can enhance cancer detection accuracy and help standardize acquisition protocols.

title: Uncertainty-aware classification and triage of structural heart disease using electrocardiography and echocardiography metrics
summary: Machine learning methods provide a methodological innovation that can help screen for cardiovascular disease through noninvasive and readily available measurement modalities. Recent investments in using electrocardiogram (ECG) data to screen for structural heart disease (SHD) are one example, where ECGs provide a low-cost, available modality for screening. This has led to the EchoNext dataset, a paired ECG-echocardiogram data repository for testing new methods of SHD detection. However, relatively few studies have investigated how more probabilistic classification through Bayesian inference may improve uncertainty quantification in this setting. Moreover, few studies have considered how triage systems can be developed to alleviate healthcare bottlenecks, such as the review of data from underserved, rural clinics by expert sonographers for SHD assessment. In this study, we leverage existing ECG-echocardiogram data to compare frequentist and Bayesian neural network classifiers. We show that the Bayesian approach is comparable or better than frequentist methods in SHD classification, and that they have a more robust uncertainty quantification attached to them. We provide an example of how this uncertainty-aware classification scheme can be used for screening SHD, providing a proof-of-concept for how machine learning can help with triage in getting individuals expert sonographer input when SHD is highly likely or measurements are highly uncertain.

AI.tldr: This study compares Bayesian vs. frequentist neural network classifiers for detecting structural heart disease from ECG data, showing Bayesian methods achieve comparable/better performance with more robust uncertainty quantification, and demonstrates a proof-of-concept triage system using uncertainty-aware classification to prioritize expert sonographer review.
AI.motivation: To improve uncertainty quantification in machine learning-based screening for structural heart disease using ECG data, and to develop triage systems that alleviate healthcare bottlenecks in underserved rural clinics by leveraging probabilistic classification.
AI.method: Compared frequentist and Bayesian neural network classifiers on the EchoNext paired ECG-echocardiogram dataset for structural heart disease detection, evaluating classification performance and uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: Bayesian neural networks achieved comparable or better classification performance than frequentist methods for SHD detection, with more robust uncertainty quantification attached to predictions.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware Bayesian classification can be effectively used for screening SHD and provides a proof-of-concept for machine learning-based triage systems that prioritize expert sonographer review when SHD is highly likely or measurements are highly uncertain.

title: Active Sensing Subserves Task-Level Control
summary: Active sensing is traditionally defined as the expenditure of energy, typically in the form of movement, for obtaining information. Here, we propose that the combination of reliance on adaptive sensors, the linkage between movement and sensing, and task-level control inevitably gives rise to the emergence of active sensing movements. In this way, active sensing is not driven by sensory goals, such as minimizing uncertainty about the state, but rather is necessary for task-level control. This hypothesis, that active sensing subserves control, is supported by both empirical data from organisms and mathematical theory. Interestingly, active sensing behaviors often occur in discrete epochs, interspersed with goal-oriented behavior. This suggests that animals switch between two behavioral modes with distinct control policies, an `explore' mode in which animals produce dynamic movements to shape sensory feedback, and an `exploit' mode in which animals produce slower compensatory movements that are directly related to achieving task goals. This strategy for feedback control that relies on adaptive sensors, active sensing, and mode switching is not commonly used in engineered systems despite being ubiquitous in biology. Engineered systems comprising state-of-the-art sensors, actuators, and mechanical designs can outperform animals with respect to ``cost functions'' such as maximum force generation, precision, and speed. Nevertheless, animals routinely achieve robust, graceful behaviors that are currently unmatched by engineered systems, suggesting that current control systems are insufficient. These insights, expressed in the language of control theory, may be critical for improving robotic sensing and control.

AI.tldr: This paper redefines active sensing as an emergent necessity for task-level control rather than a goal-driven information-seeking process, proposing that animals switch between 'explore' (active movement for shaping feedback) and 'exploit' (compensatory goal-oriented) modes, a strategy absent in engineered systems but potentially critical for improving robotics.
AI.motivation: To challenge the traditional definition of active sensing as energy expenditure for information gain, and to propose instead that it emerges from the combination of adaptive sensors, movement-sensing linkage, and task-level control as a necessity for control rather than sensory goals.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis supported by empirical data from organisms and mathematical theory, comparing biological control strategies with engineered systems.
AI.result: Active sensing behaviors occur in discrete epochs with two modes: 'explore' (dynamic movements shaping sensory feedback) and 'exploit' (slower compensatory movements for task goals). This biological strategy is not commonly used in engineered systems.
AI.conclusion: Animals' robust, graceful behaviors unmatched by engineered systems suggest current control systems are insufficient, and insights from biological active sensing could improve robotic sensing and control.

title: Integrating Cognitive Load and Embodied Cognition Theories Through Representations as Multi-Scale Attractors
summary: This article proposes a formal rapprochement between cognitive load theory and embodied cognition by reconceptualizing psychological representations as dynamic multiscale attractors within a temporal-hierarchical prediction architecture. The apparent conflict between the two theories dissolves when viewed through a complex systems lens. Cognitive load theory describes compressed representations operating at medium timescales, while embodied cognition describes fast sensorimotor loops. These two theories describe complementary, timescale-separated processes that operate simultaneously without contradiction. Drawing on dynamical systems theory, hierarchical predictive processing, and a six-node open-systems architecture, the article proposes that learning is best understood as attractor sculpting across coupled temporal layers, from millisecond sensorimotor loops through seconds-to-minutes working memory compression to the slow, years-long reshaping of knowledge structures. Three theoretical reconciliations are developed: time-scale separation, spatially extended hierarchies, and developmental trajectories from novice to expert configurations. From these understandings, five novel, testable predictions are advanced concerning cross-timescale interference, embodied load reduction, metacognition as timescale coupling, feedback topology, and the schema flexibility paradox. For each prediction, converging empirical evidence is reviewed, and formal empirical research designs are proposed. Implications for instructional design, assessment practice, and educational leadership are developed throughout, grounded in the principle that cognitive load and embodied engagement are not competing demands but complementary expressions of a unified temporal-hierarchical cognitive system.

AI.tldr: This paper reconciles cognitive load theory and embodied cognition by reconceptualizing psychological representations as dynamic multiscale attractors within a temporal-hierarchical prediction architecture, proposing that they describe complementary, timescale-separated processes (from millisecond sensorimotor loops to years-long knowledge restructuring) rather than competing theories.
AI.motivation: To resolve the apparent theoretical conflict between cognitive load theory and embodied cognition by showing they are not competing but complementary, operating at different timescales within a unified cognitive system.
AI.method: Draws on dynamical systems theory, hierarchical predictive processing, and a six-node open-systems architecture to reconceptualize representations as dynamic multiscale attractors across temporal layers.
AI.result: Develops three theoretical reconciliations (time-scale separation, spatially extended hierarchies, developmental trajectories) and five novel testable predictions (cross-timescale interference, embodied load reduction, metacognition as timescale coupling, feedback topology, schema flexibility paradox).
AI.conclusion: Cognitive load and embodied engagement are complementary expressions of a unified temporal-hierarchical cognitive system, with implications for instructional design, assessment, and educational leadership.

title: Contextual Role Modulates Object Representational Geometry in the Human Brain
summary: The human brain represents objects in a way that is both invariant across instances and flexible enough to support different contexts and tasks. Yet it remains unknown how object representations are dynamically remapped as the same object shifts across contextual roles. Here we combined fMRI with naturalistic movie viewing to investigate how the same objects are represented when they are passive elements in the scene versus the targets of goal-directed actions. When objects were action targets, they engaged a parietal action network centered in the supramarginal and postcentral gyri, while passive objects recruited a distributed occipito-temporal network involved in visual object recognition. Within the networks most strongly encoding objects in their respective contexts, representational geometry showed a double dissociation: target object representations were organized by action affordance and hand posture affordance dimensions, while passive object representations aligned with semantic dimensions. In addition, visual representational structure was invariant to context. Outside those context-specific brain networks, representational content retained context-invariance, indicating that flexibility and invariance operate at different levels of the same representational system. Together, these findings demonstrate neural remapping of object representational geometries in a manner that depends on moment-to-moment changes in the contextual relevance of objects within a naturalistic scene.

AI.tldr: This fMRI study with naturalistic movie viewing reveals that object representations in the brain are dynamically remapped based on contextual role: objects as action targets engage parietal networks organized by action affordances, while passive objects recruit occipito-temporal networks organized semantically, with visual structure remaining invariant across contexts.
AI.motivation: To understand how the brain flexibly represents the same objects differently depending on their contextual roles (passive scene elements vs. targets of goal-directed actions), bridging the gap between invariant object recognition and context-dependent neural remapping.
AI.method: Combined fMRI with naturalistic movie viewing; compared neural representations of objects when they were passive elements in scenes versus targets of goal-directed actions; analyzed representational geometry within context-specific brain networks.
AI.result: Target objects engaged a parietal action network (supramarginal/postcentral gyri) organized by action affordance dimensions; passive objects recruited occipito-temporal networks organized semantically; visual representational structure remained context-invariant; double dissociation in representational geometry across contexts.
AI.conclusion: Neural remapping of object representations occurs dynamically based on moment-to-moment contextual relevance, with flexibility and invariance operating at different levels of the same representational system.

title: Beyond Neural Activity Prediction: Probing Latent Representations in Mouse V1 Digital Twins
summary: Digital twins of sensory cortex serve as powerful response oracles. Although prediction accuracy is the central metric by which these models are evaluated, it provides limited insight into the latent representations that support those predictions. This becomes increasingly important as digital twins are used as in silico experimental systems for stimulus design and hypothesis generation: models with similar prediction accuracy may rely on different latent representations. We address this gap by systematically probing a family of digital twins of mouse V1 trained to predict neural activity from naturalistic videos recorded in freely moving mice. The models share the same training data and neural-prediction objective, but differ in visual-encoder architecture. For each frozen model, we characterize latent representations along three levels: (i) linear decodability from controlled visual probes of orientation, contrast, and motion; (ii) latent-unit tuning to canonical visual features including orientation selectivity, contrast response, spatial-frequency tuning; and (iii) population geometry of hidden-layer activity. Across architectures, better neural-response prediction correlates with stronger probe accuracy. Additionally, highly predictive models exhibit flatter hidden-population eigenspectra, indicating higher-dimensional representations closer to population-geometry signatures reported in mouse V1. Although these representational properties covary with prediction accuracy across architectures, digital twins with comparable prediction scores can still differ substantially in probe performance and latent-unit tuning. These results establish multi-level representational probing as a complement to standard neural-prediction evaluation, providing a framework for understanding digital twins not only as predictors, but also as substrates for studying visual computations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a multi-level representational probing framework to analyze digital twins of mouse V1, revealing that models with similar prediction accuracy can differ substantially in latent representations, and that better prediction correlates with higher-dimensional population geometries and stronger probe accuracy.
AI.motivation: Prediction accuracy alone provides limited insight into latent representations of digital twins, which becomes problematic as these models are used for in silico experiments. Models with similar prediction accuracy may rely on different latent representations.
AI.method: Systematically probed a family of digital twins (mouse V1) with same training data and neural-prediction objective but different visual-encoder architectures. Characterized latent representations at three levels: linear decodability from visual probes, latent-unit tuning to canonical features, and population geometry of hidden-layer activity.
AI.result: Better neural-response prediction correlates with stronger probe accuracy and flatter hidden-population eigenspectra (higher-dimensional representations). Digital twins with comparable prediction scores can still differ substantially in probe performance and latent-unit tuning.
AI.conclusion: Multi-level representational probing is a valuable complement to standard neural-prediction evaluation, offering a framework for understanding digital twins as substrates for studying visual computations, not just as predictors.

title: Asymptotic Counting of Binary Phylogenetic Networks
summary: Phylogenetic networks provide a general framework for modeling reticulate evolutionary processes such as hybridization, recombination, and horizontal gene transfer. In this paper, we study the asymptotic counting of binary phylogenetic networks with $k$ reticulations on $n$ taxa, where $k$ is allowed to grow with $n$. Using edge insertion, we analyze the local structures that affect the number of possible constructions of such networks. By bounding the contribution of networks with exceptional local configurations and combining these bounds with known asymptotic formulas for tree-child networks, we show that, when $k=o(\sqrt n)$, the number of binary phylogenetic networks with $k$ reticulations on $n$ taxa is asymptotic to \[ \binom{n}{k}2^{n+k-1/2}n^{n+k-1}e^{-n}. \]

AI.tldr: This paper derives asymptotic formulas for counting binary phylogenetic networks with k reticulations on n taxa when k grows with n, specifically when k=o(√n), showing the number is asymptotic to a binomial expression.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic counting of phylogenetic networks with reticulations, where the number of reticulations k is allowed to grow with the number of taxa n, extending previous results that were limited to fixed k.
AI.method: Using edge insertion techniques to analyze local structures affecting network construction counts, bounding contributions from networks with exceptional local configurations, and combining these bounds with known asymptotic formulas for tree-child networks.
AI.result: When k=o(√n), the number of binary phylogenetic networks with k reticulations on n taxa is asymptotic to binom(n,k) * 2^{n+k-1/2} * n^{n+k-1} * e^{-n}.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes asymptotic counting results for phylogenetic networks with growing numbers of reticulations, providing a formula that applies in the regime where k grows more slowly than the square root of n.

title: Abstract relational structures in models of biology
summary: The mathematical formalisms used to model biological systems induce both latent and ambiguous assumptions that can limit or distort their representational capabilities. Developing formalisms that can represent systems more precisely is fundamental to comprehending their intricacies and complexities. Here we introduce the systems hypergraph, a general and extendable formalism for representing abstract relational systems. A systems hypergraph combines a hypergraph, representing multidimensional relations among objects, with a hierarchical system of attributes representing system properties and their interdependencies. The attribute structure ensures that dependencies between system properties are patent and unambiguous, thereby clarifying assumptions and avoiding redundancy in data association. As an application we consider two formalisms widely used in systems biology - chemical reaction networks and stochastic Petri nets - and study their natural representation as systems hypergraphs. This allows us to relate the two formalisms rigorously, demonstrating in particular that stochastic Petri nets are strictly more general than chemical reaction networks in contrast to their commonly assumed equivalence. More broadly our work demonstrates the power of abstraction, and in particular its role in mediating between objects and relations in mathematical representations of biological complexity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "systems hypergraph," a new formalism combining hypergraphs with hierarchical attributes to model biological systems more precisely. It shows that stochastic Petri nets are strictly more general than chemical reaction networks, contradicting their assumed equivalence.
AI.motivation: Existing mathematical formalisms for biological systems contain latent and ambiguous assumptions that limit representational precision. The authors seek a formalism that can represent systems more accurately to better understand biological complexity.
AI.method: The systems hypergraph combines a hypergraph (for multidimensional relations among objects) with a hierarchical attribute system (for system properties and interdependencies). The authors apply this to chemical reaction networks and stochastic Petri nets.
AI.result: Stochastic Petri nets are strictly more general than chemical reaction networks, contradicting their commonly assumed equivalence. The attribute structure ensures dependencies between system properties are clear and unambiguous.
AI.conclusion: Abstraction is a powerful tool for mediating between objects and relations in mathematical representations of biological complexity. The systems hypergraph formalism enables rigorous comparison between different modeling approaches.

title: Tread lightly interpreting group differences in genetic risk
summary: Observed differences in mean phenotypic values across human groups have attracted renewed interest with the rise of large-scale genomic studies and polygenic risk prediction. However, the genetic basis of these differences is far more difficult to establish than is often appreciated. Populations can diverge in allele frequency differences without diverging in mean genetic value. Empirical approaches to infer whether populations differ in mean genetic value fall under two broad categories: top-down approaches, which quantify the proportion of phenotypic variance explained by ancestry and bottom-up approaches, which compare polygenic scores across groups. However, both approaches have limitations that prevent them from reliably distinguishing true differences in genetic apart from statistical artifacts like population structure, ascertainment bias, and poor cross-ancestry portability. Further, observed phenotypic shifts between populations may reflect bias in phenotype measurement and heterogeneity in study design rather than underlying genetic drivers. We argue that claims about group differences in genetic risk should be interpreted with considerable caution.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that claims about genetic differences in mean phenotypic values across human groups are unreliable due to methodological limitations, statistical artifacts, and measurement biases in both top-down and bottom-up approaches.
AI.motivation: To address the renewed interest in observed phenotypic differences across human groups from large-scale genomic studies, and to highlight that the genetic basis of these differences is far harder to establish than commonly assumed.
AI.method: The paper reviews two broad empirical approaches—top-down (quantifying phenotypic variance explained by ancestry) and bottom-up (comparing polygenic scores across groups)—and analyzes their limitations including population structure, ascertainment bias, and poor cross-ancestry portability.
AI.result: Both top-down and bottom-up approaches fail to reliably distinguish true genetic differences from statistical artifacts, and phenotypic shifts may reflect measurement bias and study design heterogeneity rather than genetic drivers.
AI.conclusion: Claims about group differences in genetic risk should be interpreted with considerable caution due to multiple sources of bias and artifact.

title: PRAXIS: Case-distilled and code-verified AI agents for biological research
summary: Large language models are moving scientific research from text assistance toward agentic workflows, yet biological research requires strong object validation, methodological suitability, reproducibility, and auditability. Prompt engineering, general RAG, or tool use alone cannot reliably produce domain-specific scientific judgment. Here, we present PRAXIS, a verifiable biological research agent framework driven by literature learning and case distillation. PRAXIS converts research experience, failure boundaries, domain rules, and executable procedures into structured long-term memory. By coordinating successful cases, negative cases, rules, and skills, PRAXIS supports problem definition, object validation, method selection, workflow execution, result interpretation, and review feedback across diverse biocomputational tasks. We instantiated PRAXIS as an agent suite for biomedical computing and evaluated it through object validation, case retrieval, memory ablation, public benchmarks, and cross-agent workflows. The results show that case-based learning improves method selection, error suppression, and workflow organization in complex biological research tasks. Rather than replacing scientists, PRAXIS provides a general pathway for transforming research experience into executable, auditable, and transferable agent capabilities.

AI.tldr: PRAXIS is a verifiable biological research agent framework that uses literature learning and case distillation to convert research experience into structured long-term memory, enabling domain-specific scientific judgment across diverse biocomputational tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs alone cannot reliably produce domain-specific scientific judgment in biological research due to the need for object validation, methodological suitability, reproducibility, and auditability.
AI.method: PRAXIS converts research experience, failure boundaries, domain rules, and executable procedures into structured long-term memory by coordinating successful cases, negative cases, rules, and skills to support problem definition, object validation, method selection, workflow execution, result interpretation, and review feedback.
AI.result: Case-based learning improves method selection, error suppression, and workflow organization in complex biological research tasks, as validated through object validation, case retrieval, memory ablation, public benchmarks, and cross-agent workflows.
AI.conclusion: PRAXIS provides a general pathway for transforming research experience into executable, auditable, and transferable agent capabilities without replacing scientists.

title: Population-Specific Genetic and Non-Genetic Influences on Sleep Traits and Health Outcomes
summary: Sleep traits are shaped by genetic and environmental factors and may influence many health conditions. The All of Us Research Program, which includes EHR, physical measurements, genomic data, and wearable data across ancestry groups, provides an opportunity to study genetic and non-genetic contributors to sleep-related health outcomes. We examined associations between genetic predispositions to chronotype, sleep duration, and short sleep and health outcomes across ancestries, as well as the role of measured sleep duration. We used All of Us genome-wide association study results, including ancestry-specific and meta-analyses for 3,414 phenotypes, to identify phenotypes associated with 455 sleep-related SNPs. Cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses (n = 212,529) evaluated associations between polygenic risk scores (PRS) and anthropometric and metabolic measures from EHR. A subgroup analysis (n = 7,655) assessed sleep duration using Fitbit data. Across six ancestry groups, SNP analysis identified 61 phenotypes linked to 29 sleep-trait-associated SNPs. The chronotype SNP rs1421085 in FTO showed the strongest associations with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular conditions, mainly in European, American, and African groups. PRS analysis showed that higher predisposition to shorter sleep duration was associated with increased risk of obesity and diabetes, with ancestry-specific variation. Measured sleep duration attenuated these associations, with relative contributions of 85.6%-99.9% in cross-sectional analyses and 7.1%-44.0% in longitudinal analyses compared with PRS. This study identified health conditions associated with genetic predispositions to sleep traits and suggests that actual sleep duration may play a prominent role in sleep-related health outcomes. Differences among meta-, pooled-, and ancestry-specific analyses highlight the importance of population-specific research.

AI.tldr: This study used All of Us data across six ancestry groups to examine how genetic predispositions to sleep traits (chronotype, sleep duration, short sleep) relate to 3,414 health phenotypes. Key findings include: the FTO rs1421085 SNP linked to obesity/diabetes/cardiovascular conditions; higher PRS for short sleep associated with increased obesity and diabetes risk; and measured sleep duration (from Fitbit) significantly attenuated PRS-health associations (85.6-99.9% cross-sectionally, 7.1-44.0% longitudinally), highlighting the importance of actual sleep duration over genetic predisposition alone.
AI.motivation: To leverage the All of Us Research Program's diverse data (EHR, genomics, wearables) across ancestry groups to disentangle genetic and non-genetic contributors to sleep-related health outcomes, addressing gaps in population-specific sleep research.
AI.method: GWAS analysis for 3,414 phenotypes across six ancestry groups using 455 sleep-related SNPs; cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses (n=212,529) examining associations between polygenic risk scores (PRS) and anthropometric/metabolic measures; subgroup analysis with Fitbit-measured sleep duration (n=7,655).
AI.result: Identified 61 phenotypes linked to 29 sleep-trait SNPs; rs1421085 (FTO) strongly associated with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular conditions in European, American, and African groups; higher PRS for short sleep associated with increased obesity/diabetes risk with ancestry-specific variation; measured sleep duration attenuated PRS associations by 85.6-99.9% (cross-sectional) and 7.1-44.0% (longitudinal).
AI.conclusion: Genetic predispositions to sleep traits are linked to multiple health conditions, but actual measured sleep duration plays a prominent mediating role. Ancestry-specific and meta-analyses differences underscore the need for population-specific research in sleep genetics.

title: On the Design of an Analog-Dyadic Converter CRN
summary: The Chemical Reaction Networks (CRN) interpreted through the differential semantics, even when restricted to elementary reactions with mass action law kinetics, form a Turing-complete language. This means that any computable real function can thus be programmed, and in fact compiled, in an abstract CRN that will compute it with an arbitrarily high precision. In this computational framework, the information carriers are the molecular concentrations, the required precision is given as input, and the output concentration is guaranteed to satisfy the required precision. On the other hand, one can be interested in estimating the derivative of an unknown input signal or in reading the concentration value of an input molecular species. By nature, such problems can only be approximated with a finite precision. Hence, the computation framework proposed previously cannot be applied and we need to design and analyze custom CRNs to perform these tasks. In this paper, we present an analog-dyadic converter CRN which takes as input one molecular concentration (in [0, 1] but not necessarily computable), and produces as output a sequence of ''on'' and ''off'' spikes corresponding to some extent to the sequence of bits in the dyadic representation of the input concentration. We provide a detailed analysis of the source of errors and their behavior when varying the reactions rate constants. We conclude by sketching a possible design for a reader module that takes as input an arbitrary concentration and a desired precision and outputs a dyadic encoding approximating the value of the concentration with the desired precision. We leave as an open question to prove the correctness of our construction.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an analog-dyadic converter CRN that converts molecular concentrations into a sequence of "on/off" spikes representing dyadic bits, designed for reading/derivative estimation of input signals where standard Turing-complete CRN approaches don't apply.
AI.motivation: Standard CRNs are Turing-complete for computable functions with arbitrary precision, but cannot handle problems like reading unknown input concentrations or estimating derivatives of input signals, which can only be approximated with finite precision.
AI.method: An analog-dyadic converter CRN that takes a molecular concentration (in [0,1], not necessarily computable) and outputs a sequence of "on/off" spikes corresponding to the dyadic representation bits of the input concentration. The design includes error analysis under varying reaction rate constants and a sketched reader module for arbitrary precision encoding.
AI.result: Detailed analysis of error sources and their behavior when reaction rate constants vary. A possible design for a reader module that outputs dyadic encoding of input concentration with desired precision is sketched.
AI.conclusion: The correctness of the construction remains an open question, requiring future proof verification.

title: From Simulation to Discovery: AI Enabled Probabilistic Emulation of Mechanistic Crop Systems
summary: Global food security depends on predicting crop responses to climate variability, yet process based crop models remain too computationally expensive for large scale exploration of genotype and environment interactions. Here we develop a probabilistic neural emulator of APSIM that reproduces key maize growth processes across 13 outputs with high fidelity (with R^2 of 0.93) while reducing simulation time by several orders of magnitude. Trained on two million simulations spanning diverse genetic, soil, and management conditions, and augmented with a convolutional synthetic weather generator that produces physically consistent climate sequences, the framework enables scalable exploration of crop responses under realistic and diverse environmental inputs while providing calibrated predictive uncertainty without costly Bayesian inference. Applying this framework across 100,000 trait configurations, six soil environments in Iowa and Illinois, and climate projections through the year 2100 under two emissions scenarios, we identify 181 maize trait combinations that consistently maintain high yield across all tested conditionsan analysis infeasible with the mechanistic model alone. We further show that radiation use efficiency and temperature driven root dynamics are dominant drivers of yield resilience. Notably, projected yield distributions vary substantially across locations, with some lower productivity sites exhibiting yield increases under future climate scenarios, indicating that climate change may reshape regional yield potential in nonintuitive ways. These results demonstrate how uncertainty aware emulation transforms mechanistic crop simulation from a computational bottleneck into an on demand discovery engine, one capable of interrogating the full genotype, environment and management space at a scale no process-based model can match.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic neural emulator of the APSIM crop model is developed, trained on 2 million simulations, achieving high accuracy (R²=0.93) while being orders of magnitude faster, enabling large-scale exploration of maize genotype × environment interactions under future climate scenarios.
AI.motivation: Process-based crop models are too computationally expensive for large-scale exploration of genotype and environment interactions, limiting our ability to predict crop responses to climate variability and ensure global food security.
AI.method: A probabilistic neural emulator of APSIM was trained on 2 million simulations across diverse genetic, soil, and management conditions, augmented with a convolutional synthetic weather generator producing physically consistent climate sequences, enabling scalable exploration with calibrated predictive uncertainty.
AI.result: The emulator achieved R² of 0.93 across 13 outputs, identified 181 maize trait combinations maintaining high yield across all tested conditions, and revealed that radiation use efficiency and temperature-driven root dynamics are dominant drivers of yield resilience. Some lower-productivity sites showed yield increases under future climate scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware emulation transforms mechanistic crop simulation from a computational bottleneck into an on-demand discovery engine capable of interrogating the full genotype, environment, and management space at a scale no process-based model can match.

title: Topological Signal Processing: An Application-Oriented Tutorial
summary: Many modern datasets are large and carry complex structural relationships. Graph-based methods have traditionally been used to represent networked data, modeling individual elements as nodes and pairwise interactions as edges. Furthermore, Graph Signal Processing (GSP) has been developed to analyze signals on graph nodes, such as temperature measurements (node signals) across different regions of a country represented as a graph. Topological Signal Processing (TSP) is an emerging field that generalizes GSP, enabling the analysis of signals defined not only on nodes but also on edges, triangles, and higher-dimensional network elements, modeled as simplicial complexes and related topological structures. This makes TSP naturally well-suited for studying higher-order interactions in complex systems by extending classical signal processing concepts, such as filtering and Fourier transforms, to the topological level. Despite its versatility, TSP remains challenging for many practitioners. Therefore, we present an accessible overview of TSP foundations while drawing connections with application-oriented settings. We focus on processing techniques based on the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian, which generalizes the graph Laplacian to simplicial complexes. In particular, we review key TSP concepts, relate them to real-world examples, and discuss how higher-order structures and signals can be derived from datasets. For instance, we introduce an edge-level signal capturing lagged interactions between nodal signals, and demonstrate its use in a case study on TSP-based analysis of brain imaging data, revealing nontrivial interactions between sets of brain regions. Overall, we aim to promote a broader adoption of TSP by bridging methodological developments with applications, fostering its use among a wide community of theoretical and applied researchers.

AI.tldr: This paper provides an accessible overview of Topological Signal Processing (TSP), which extends Graph Signal Processing to higher-order structures (edges, triangles, etc.) using the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian. It connects TSP foundations to real-world applications, including a case study on brain imaging data, aiming to promote broader adoption of TSP methods.
AI.motivation: Many modern datasets have complex structural relationships that go beyond simple pairwise interactions. While Graph Signal Processing (GSP) handles signals on nodes, it cannot capture higher-order interactions on edges, triangles, etc. Topological Signal Processing (TSP) addresses this gap but remains challenging for practitioners, motivating the need for an accessible overview bridging theory and applications.
AI.method: The paper presents a tutorial-style overview of TSP foundations based on the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian (which generalizes the graph Laplacian to simplicial complexes). It introduces key TSP concepts (filtering, Fourier transforms at the topological level) and demonstrates how higher-order signals can be derived from data—e.g., edge-level signals capturing lagged interactions between nodal signals. A case study on brain imaging data is used for illustration.
AI.result: The paper successfully demonstrates TSP's applicability through a brain imaging case study, revealing nontrivial higher-order interactions between sets of brain regions that would be missed by standard graph-based methods. It also provides concrete examples of constructing higher-order signals from real datasets.
AI.conclusion: TSP is a powerful generalization of GSP for analyzing signals on higher-order network structures. By providing accessible explanations and real-world examples, the authors aim to promote broader adoption of TSP among both theoretical and applied researchers, bridging the gap between methodological advances and practical applications.

title: FederatedRSF : Federated Random Survival Forests for Partially Overlapping Medical Data
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures. Maryam Moradpour, Jonas Harriehausen, and Amirreza Aleyasin contributed equally to this work. Includes supplementary material
summary: Multi-center survival prediction can improve robustness and generalizability, yet privacy regulations and institutional governance often prevent pooling patient-level clinical and genomic data across institutions. In practice, deployment is further complicated by feature-space heterogeneity, in which sites collect different covariates or use different sequencing panels, resulting in only partially overlapping feature sets. We present FederatedRSF, a Python package that implements federated random survival forests, aggregating locally trained survival trees and redistributing only feature-compatible trees to each site, enabling inference with partial overlap without sharing raw data. We evaluate FederatedRSF on the GBSG2 breast cancer cohort distributed with the scikit-survival package, simulating feature heterogeneity across clients by withholding subsets of features, and assessing discrimination using Harrell's concordance index (C-Index) under repeated cross-validation and site-splits. The results demonstrated that the federated model can achieve performance comparable to that of the centralized training setting.

AI.tldr: FederatedRSF is a Python package for federated random survival forests that handles feature-space heterogeneity across institutions without sharing raw data, achieving performance comparable to centralized training.
AI.motivation: Privacy regulations and institutional governance prevent pooling patient-level data across institutions for multi-center survival prediction; deployment is further complicated by feature-space heterogeneity (different covariates or sequencing panels across sites).
AI.method: The package implements federated random survival forests by aggregating locally trained survival trees and redistributing only feature-compatible trees to each site, enabling inference with partial feature overlap without sharing raw data.
AI.result: Evaluated on the GBSG2 breast cancer cohort, the federated model achieved performance comparable to centralized training, as measured by Harrell's C-Index under repeated cross-validation and site-splits.
AI.conclusion: FederatedRSF enables privacy-preserving multi-center survival prediction even with heterogeneous feature sets across sites, achieving centralized-level performance.

title: GazeBehavior Annotation Toolkit (GBAT): AI-powered toolkit for automatic annotation of egocentric eye-tracking and video data of child-caregiver interaction
comment: submitted to IEEE International Conference on Development and Learning (ICDL), 2026
summary: Video recordings of child-caregiver interactions enable investigation of attentional dynamics during naturalistic behavior. Such multimodal recording also allows researchers to examine how attention interacts with action and language use in real time. However, manual annotation of such data is time-consuming. Here, we introduce GazeBehavior Annotation Toolkit, a deep-learning-based toolkit designed to facilitate three key processes in data preprocessing and feature extraction: post-hoc synchronization across multiple videos, semi-automatic annotation of gaze target categories, and categorization of participants' poses and hand actions. This toolkit improves the efficiency and scalability of feature extraction from human egocentric eye-tracking and video data. Such improvement is critical in supporting large-scale and longitudinal investigations of attentional dynamics and naturalistic behavior in human early development.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GazeBehavior Annotation Toolkit, a deep-learning toolkit for semi-automatic annotation of gaze, pose, and hand actions from child-caregiver interaction videos, improving efficiency for large-scale developmental studies.
AI.motivation: Manual annotation of video recordings of child-caregiver interactions is time-consuming, limiting large-scale and longitudinal studies of attentional dynamics in naturalistic behavior.
AI.method: A deep-learning-based toolkit that performs post-hoc video synchronization, semi-automatic gaze target categorization, and classification of participants' poses and hand actions from egocentric eye-tracking and video data.
AI.result: The toolkit improves efficiency and scalability of feature extraction from human egocentric eye-tracking and video data.
AI.conclusion: This toolkit supports large-scale and longitudinal investigations of attentional dynamics and naturalistic behavior in early human development.

title: Brain-LLM Alignment Tracks Training Data, Not Typology
comment: Accepted to CoNLL 2026. 9 pages main content + 4 pages references + 6 pages appendix; 4 figures, 13 tables
summary: Brain-LLM alignment is well established in English, yet the brain's language network is neuroanatomically universal across languages. Does alignment also generalize cross-linguistically, and what governs the variation? We test this using fMRI data from 112 participants across English, Chinese, and French (the Le Petit Prince corpus) and seven LLMs spanning English-dominant, Chinese-dominant, and multilingual architectures. Our central finding is that training-language dominance, not an inherent property of English, drives the alignment pattern: a Chinese-dominant model (Baichuan2-7B), architecture-matched to LLaMA-2-7B, reverses the gradient entirely, aligning best with Chinese brains and worst with English. Beyond training dominance, formal typological distance independently covaries with alignment degradation, syntax-associated brain regions (IFG) show $2.3\times$ steeper typological gradients than lexico-semantic regions (PTL), and tokenization fertility accounts for $\sim$60% of a cross-linguistic shift in optimal encoding layer. These results reveal that the apparent "English advantage" in brain-LLM alignment is an artifact of training data composition, while the remaining variation reflects genuine typological structure concentrated in syntactic processing.

AI.tldr: Brain-LLM alignment is not inherently better for English; training-language dominance drives alignment patterns, with typological distance and tokenization fertility explaining cross-linguistic variation in neural encoding.
AI.motivation: Previous work established brain-LLM alignment in English, but the brain's language network is universal across languages. This paper investigates whether alignment generalizes cross-linguistically and what factors govern the observed variation.
AI.method: Used fMRI data from 112 participants across English, Chinese, and French (Le Petit Prince corpus) and tested seven LLMs with different language dominances (English-dominant, Chinese-dominant, multilingual). Analyzed training-language dominance, typological distance, brain region specificity (IFG vs. PTL), and tokenization fertility.
AI.result: Training-language dominance drives alignment (Chinese-dominant model reverses the gradient, aligning best with Chinese). Typological distance independently correlates with alignment degradation, syntax regions (IFG) show 2.3× steeper gradients than lexico-semantic regions (PTL), and tokenization fertility explains ~60% of cross-linguistic shift in optimal encoding layer.
AI.conclusion: The apparent "English advantage" in brain-LLM alignment is an artifact of training data composition; remaining variation reflects genuine typological structure, particularly in syntactic processing.

title: Sparse Autoencoders Map Brain-LLM Alignment onto Cortical Semantic Topography
comment: Accepted at CoNLL 2026. 20 pages (9 main + 1 limitations/acknowledgments + 3 references + 7 appendix), 5 figures, 20 tables
summary: Intermediate layers of large language models (LLMs) best predict human brain responses to language, one of the most robust findings in computational neurolinguistics, yet why remains mechanistically unexplained. We address this gap by bridging sparse autoencoders (SAEs) from mechanistic interpretability with neural encoding models, decomposing GPT-2 XL and Llama-3.1-8B into 16K-32K interpretable features per layer. A human-validated taxonomy ($κ\geq 0.74$) reveals that semantic features alone recover 94% of peak encoding performance ($r=0.285$), substantially exceeding variance-matched baselines ($p<0.001$, $d=1.31$). Beyond this aggregate dominance, we test a novel cortical topography prediction: five semantic subcategories derived a priori from three independent neuroscience programs should map onto distinct brain regions. A formal convergence test confirms this alignment (Spearman $ρ=0.72$, $p<0.001$; hypergeometric $p=0.007$), demonstrating that SAE-discovered features recapitulate known cortical semantic organization at a granularity inaccessible to prior methods. SAE features further predict human reading times beyond lexical controls ($Δ\mathrm{logLik}=38.4$, $p<0.001$), and an exploratory prediction-error analysis provides preliminary evidence that the brain additionally encodes unexpected semantic content. Results generalize across English, Chinese, and French.

AI.tldr: This paper explains why intermediate LLM layers best predict brain responses: sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose models into interpretable features, showing semantic features dominate (94% of peak encoding), predict cortical topography (5 semantic subcategories map to distinct brain regions), and explain reading times beyond lexical controls, generalizing across English, Chinese, and French.
AI.motivation: To mechanistically explain the well-known finding that intermediate LLM layers best predict human brain responses to language, by bridging mechanistic interpretability (sparse autoencoders) with neural encoding models.
AI.method: Decompose GPT-2 XL and Llama-3.1-8B into 16K-32K interpretable features per layer using sparse autoencoders; validate with a human taxonomy (κ≥0.74); test cortical topography predictions against three independent neuroscience programs; evaluate reading time prediction and prediction-error analysis across English, Chinese, and French.
AI.result: Semantic features recover 94% of peak encoding performance (r=0.285), significantly exceeding baselines (p<0.001, d=1.31); cortical topography alignment confirmed (Spearman ρ=0.72, p<0.001; hypergeometric p=0.007); SAE features predict reading times beyond lexical controls (ΔlogLik=38.4, p<0.001); results generalize across three languages.
AI.conclusion: The mechanistic explanation is that intermediate layers encode rich semantic representations—discoverable via SAEs—which are the primary drivers of brain alignment, with evidence for cortical semantic organization and unexpected content encoding.

title: Geometric Origin of Exact Mean-Field Reductions: M{ö}bius Symmetry and the Lorentzian Ansatz
summary: Low-dimensional descriptions of large systems of coupled oscillators and spiking neurons rely heavily on the Lorentzian Ansatz. We show that its privileged role is geometric rather than heuristic: for the transport induced by Riccati dynamics, the Cauchy-Lorentz family indeed emerges as the unique connected two-dimensional family of continuous probability densities that is invariant under the induced projective transport. The key step of the demonstration is to reformulate the dynamics on the circle, where the problem reduces to the uniqueness of the rotation-invariant probability measure. Under stereographic projection, this yields the standard Cauchy law and, under the full projective action, the Lorentzian family. This result gives a unified geometric foundation for the Ott-Antonsen [Chaos 18, 037113 (2008)] and Montbri{ó}-Paz{ó}-Roxin [Phys. Rev. X 5, 021028 (2015)] reductions, explains the failure of Gaussian closures, and identifies the structural condition underlying exact two-parameter reductions.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that the Cauchy-Lorentz family is the unique connected two-dimensional family of probability densities invariant under Riccati-driven projective transport, providing a geometric foundation for the Ott-Antonsen and Montbrió-Pazó-Roxin reductions in oscillator and spiking neuron models.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous geometric justification for why the Lorentzian ansatz works so effectively in reductions of large coupled oscillator and spiking neuron systems, rather than treating it as a mere heuristic choice.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the dynamics on the circle, reducing the problem to uniqueness of rotation-invariant probability measures. Using stereographic projection and projective action, they show the Cauchy-Lorentz family emerges naturally as the invariant family under Riccati-transport dynamics.
AI.result: The Cauchy-Lorentz family is the unique connected two-dimensional family of continuous probability densities invariant under the projective transport induced by Riccati dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The Lorentzian ansatz has a geometric (not heuristic) foundation, which explains the success of Ott-Antonsen and Montbrió-Pazó-Roxin reductions, clarifies why Gaussian closures fail, and identifies the structural condition for exact two-parameter reductions.

title: Particle Image Velocimetry of 3D printed vascular fluidic phantom devices
summary: Altered hemodynamics play a key role in cerebrovascular diseases such as aneurysms and stenosis. However, in vivo imaging lacks the spatial resolution required to resolve flow dynamics in small vessels. This study presents an experimental framework to investigate microscale hemodynamics using transparent 3D printed vascular models and particle image velocimetry (PIV). Optically transparent microfluidic models with straight and pathological (aneurysmal and stenotic) geometries were fabricated via additive manufacturing up to a minimum diameter size of 500 microns and characterized using optical microscopy. Flow experiments were conducted under steady laminar conditions, and local velocity fields and wall shear stress (WSS) were measured using microPIV. Measured velocities have been compared with analytical Hagen Poiseuille predictions, obtaining mean relative errors of 5 to 17 percent. The platform reliably captured key flow features and spatial variations in velocity. Overall, the results demonstrate that transparent 3D printed vascular models combined with microPIV provide a robust experimental approach for studying microscale cerebrovascular hemodynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an experimental framework using transparent 3D printed vascular models (down to 500µm) and microPIV to study microscale hemodynamics in healthy, aneurysmal, and stenotic geometries, achieving 5-17% error against analytical predictions.
AI.motivation: In vivo imaging lacks sufficient spatial resolution to resolve flow dynamics in small cerebral vessels; a high-resolution experimental platform is needed to study altered hemodynamics in cerebrovascular diseases like aneurysms and stenosis.
AI.method: Transparent 3D printed microfluidic models (straight, aneurysmal, stenotic; min. 500µm diameter) were fabricated via additive manufacturing, characterized by optical microscopy, and tested under steady laminar flow using microPIV to measure velocity fields and wall shear stress (WSS).
AI.result: Measured velocities agreed with Hagen-Poiseuille predictions (mean relative errors 5-17%). The platform successfully captured key flow features and spatial velocity variations in pathological geometries.
AI.conclusion: Transparent 3D printed vascular models combined with microPIV offer a robust experimental approach for studying microscale cerebrovascular hemodynamics.
